name: Dimitri Tiomkin
abstract: Dimitri Zinovievich Tiomkin (May 10, 1894 – November 11, 1979) was a Russian and American film composer and conductor. Classically trained in Saint Petersburg before the Bolshevik Revolution, he moved to Berlin and then New York City after the Russian Revolution.

works.t: Piano Sonata No. 4 in F-sharp Major, Op. 30
La valse, poème chorégraphique pour piano
The Tale of Tsar Saltan: Flight of the Bumblebee

about_quotes.speaker: Quincy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: who are my friends. Broni[slaw] Kaper. Dimitri Tiomkin. All of that stuff. Miklós Rózsa. A lot of the guys, eventually we became friends.
about_quotes.doc_title: Quincy Jones — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Theodore Saunders
about_quotes.evidence: But these local arrangers and things for -- what was his name, Tompkin? ... Yeah, right, and so he did some things -- he was a great musician.

about_quotes.speaker: Jon Hendricks
about_quotes.evidence: Ned Washington had won two Academy Awards... Benny Carter was a ghost writer for Ned Washington – for Dimitri Tiomkin, who did the music.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon Hendricks — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Benny Golson
about_quotes.evidence: They were so impressed at Twentieth-Century Fox, because this is what the oldtimers did, Dimitri Tiomkin and so forth.
about_quotes.doc_title: Benny Golson — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: The old guard especially Dmitri Tiomkin and those people did not like something like this come along.
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History

about_quotes.speaker: Mandel Johnny
about_quotes.evidence: No, that started a long time ago in 1952 with the title song when Dimitri Tiomkin wrote, “High Noon.”
about_quotes.doc_title: Mandel Johnny — Smithsonian Jazz Oral History
